title: Canada's 2026 FIFA World Cup Roster
original_text:
For the first time ever, the Canadian men are set to play at consecutive FIFA World Cups and this time on home soil. When Canada takes to the pitch on June 12 in Toronto, they will be just the 19th nation to have the privilege of playing in a World Cup game in front of home fans. This will not be a Canada side just happy to be among the 48 teams in the tournament. Jesse Marsch's men are looking to make noise.
For Canada to win its first match at a World Cup and reach the knockouts, it will take a focused and committed performance. Marsch has selected a squad capable of doing just that. These are the 26 men tasked with taking Canadian soccer to new heights.
Maxime Crépeau
Orlando City (Greenfield Park, Que.)
Only weeks before it was set to begin, Maxime Crépeau was cruelly ruled out of the 2022 FIFA World Cup in Qatar when he broke his leg during the MLS Cup Final that his Los Angeles FC would go on to win on penalties. Now set for his first trip to the quadrennial tournament, the 32-year-old Crépeau is the most senior of Canada's three goalkeeping options. Capped 30 times by Canada, Crépeau will vie with Dayne St. Clair for the No. 1 shirt.
Owen Goodman
Barnsley, on loan from Crystal Palace (London)
Being the third goalkeeper on a World Cup roster isn't the easiest role in the world, but Owen Goodman is ready if his moment does come. The Crystal Palace academy product is still only 22 and yet to make his senior debut with the Eagles, but he has plenty of experience under his belt with loan spells at the likes of AFC Wimbledon, Huddersfield Town and Barnsley.
"It's just a goalkeepers' union," Goodman told TSN of his relationship with Dayne St. Clair and Maxime Crépeau. "It's just like being one big group. Look, I'm obviously the new kid here. I'm not coming in expecting to play straight away or anything. I'm here to learn. I'm here to be around a good group of players and just get better."
Dayne St. Clair
Inter Miami (Pickering, Ont.)
Dayne St. Clair's rise in Major League Soccer now sees him as the first-choice goalkeeper with defending champions Lionel Messi and Inter Miami. The 29-year-old St. Clair knows all about pressure and expectations. Having appeared 19 times for Canada since his senior debut in 2021, St. Clair has rivalled Crépeau for the starter's gloves for the past several years. But despite their mutual desire to be Canada's No. 1, both men just want what's best for the country.
"For whoever Jesse picks, the other person always supports in that role and, of course, it’s difficult at times because it’s no secret we’re both competitors and we want to be the one who play," St. Clair said of Crépeau. But at the same time, I think there’s a lot of respect between us and I think that’s why we’ve been able to handle the situation so well with the respect we have for each other as people.”
Moïse Bombito
Nice (Montreal)
Since his senior debut in 2023, Moïse Bombito has moved quickly to establish himself as a foundational piece of the CanMNT and, arguably, its most important player. Forming a formidable partnership in central defence with Derek Cornelius, the 26-year-old Montreal native marshals the back with aplomb and is a calming presence in front of his goalkeeper. Bombito played every minute of Canada's run to the 2024 Copa America semis.
But like so many of his teammates, injuries were the story of Bombito's 2025-2026 campaign. Shortly after recovering from a broken wrist that delayed the start of the season, the Nice defender fractured his tibia in October that ended his season after two matches. That Bombito is ready for the World Cup is a minor miracle.
Derek Cornelius
Rangers, on loan from Marseille (Ajax, Ont.)
Derek Cornelius enters the FIFA World Cup with a point to prove. The 28-year-old centre-back finished his season, on loan at Rangers from Marseille, sidelined, but not because of injury. Recovering from a significant hamstring injury in February, Cornelius did not end up appearing in another game for Danny Rohl's side, forced to train with the reserves.
“I felt very disrespected,” Cornelius told TSN's Matt Scianitti.
The Ajax, Ont. native will now look to turn the page at his second World Cup. Alongside Moïse Bombito, Cornelius has been an effective shepherd in the middle of Marsch's backline. If Canada is to hit the heights it believes it's capable of, it will need a confident performance from Cornelius.
Luc de Fougerolles
Dender, on loan from Fulham (London)
The youngest player on Canada's roster, things have been moving quickly for Luc de Fougerolles. The 20-year-old Fulham academy product just completed his first professional season on loan at Belgian side Dender. It was a stark learning experience with the side relegated from the Pro League.
Capped for the first time in 2024, de Fougerolles became firmly ensconced in the national setup thanks to his versatility. Now having made 11 appearances for Canada, de Fougerolles is comfortable in either full-back role, in the centre of defence or in the midfield. It's that kind of flexibility that has made Marsch such a fan.
Alistair Johnston
Celtic (Vancouver)
After a frustrating season riddled with injuries, Vancouver's Alistair Johnston returned in time for a thrilling finish to his campaign. Out since October following hamstring surgery, the 27-year-old right-back came back to the Celtic lineup in late April. Johnston got back in time to help the Hoops capture an improbable Scottish Premiership title on the final matchday of the season with a 3-0 win over Hearts. Celtic followed that up with a Scottish Cup triumph over Dunfermline to make it nine major honours for Johnston over his four seasons in Glasgow.
A rock on Marsch's backline when available, Johnston was named to the team of the tournament at Copa America 2024. His steadying presence will be called upon again at the World Cup.
Alfie Jones
Middlesbrough (Bristol, England)
Eligible to play for Canada through his grandmother, Alfie Jones did whatever he could to make that happen. After a series of frustrating visa delays, the 28-year-old Middlesbrough defender made his Canada bow in a friendly with Venezuela last fall. He played the entire 90 minutes in the 2-0 victory.
Jones's club season came to an abrupt end on Boxing Day. Having appeared in all of Boro's matches and all but 24 minutes of gametime, Jones incurred a severe ankle injury during a scoreless draw with Blackburn Rovers. The injury required surgery and Jones was forced to watch Boro's run to the Championship playoff final from the sidelines. Now healthy enough to compete, the Championship veteran brings an edge to Marsch's backline and offers the kind of nastiness that opponents hate to come up against from a centre-back.
Richie Laryea
Toronto FC (Toronto)
Capped 73 times, the 31-year-old Richie Laryea is now one of Canada's elder statesmen. While his versatility is a key strength with Laryea being able to be deployed at multiple positions, his calling card is his habitual line-stepping. Laryea has an innate ability to frustrate opponents and get under their skin to bait them into bad decisions. Few are able to wield the dark arts as well as Laryea does.
"He's our Kevin Garnett," Ismaël Koné says of Laryea. "He's the ultimate trash talker, but when he has the ball and it's time to do something, he will do something. He'll win a foul, he will go forward, he will wiggle past you. Then when you come against him, it's hell."
Niko Sigur
Hajduk Split (Burnaby, BC)
Another of Jesse Marsch's Swiss Army knives, Niko Sigur will have the opportunity to play in a World Cup match in his hometown of Vancouver. After previously representing Croatia at the U21 level, the 22-year-old Sigur made the switch to his country of birth and has been capped 17 times since. Sigur is frequently deployed as a defensive midfielder at the club level, but has also shown prowess at right-back. Like others on the team, Sigur has the kind of adaptability that Marsch prizes.
Not only will playing in front of friends and family be a dream come true for Sigur, he hopes it will inspire those who come after him with proof that Canadian kids can make it.
"What was very difficult is that the Canadian players didn't have too many big names to look at, at big clubs," Sigur told TSN. "And I think that was the struggle to say, 'Okay, I believe in myself, but who did it before me?' Obviously now, when you see guys like Alphonso Davies, Jonathan David and Tajon Buchanan playing at some of the best clubs in the world, you say, 'Okay, these guys are doing it. I can definitely do it.' We just have to keep believing and, hopefully, we'll have more players coming up in big clubs."
Joel Waterman
Chicago Fire (Surrey, BC)
Heading to a second World Cup, Joel Waterman is a meat-and-potatoes kind of defender in the middle of the backline. Capable of both winning balls back and retaining them, Waterman couples fine passing with a keen defensive mind. The 30-year-old Waterman became the first-ever Canadian Premier League player to join an MLS side when he signed for CF Montreal in 2020.
While the competition for playing time among Canada's defensive corps is fierce, Waterman has proven time and again to be able to step up when called upon and offers the kind of dependability that makes him indispensable. Now in his second season with the Chicago Fire, Waterman has turned in full 90-minute shifts in eight of the 12 matches he's played across all competitions. Waterman also played all 90 minutes in Canada's 2-2 draw with Iceland in March.
Ali Ahmed
Norwich City (Toronto)
When Ali Ahmed arrived at Norwich City this past January, he thought he was in for a relegation battle. The 25-year-old Toronto-born winger found the Canaries in the drop zone and it appeared as if his mission in the Championship would be to help the club avoid playing in League One next season. But things changed in a hurry for Ahmed and Norwich. Remarkably, they rocketed out of relegation trouble and even flirted with a playoff spot before finishing the season ninth in the table.
A product of the Vancouver Whitecaps academy, Ahmed offered 15 goal contributions last season as Vancouver reached the MLS Cup Final. A stylish two-footed winger, Ahmed loves to attack and is more than a handful to defenders unsure of which way he's about to break. A player of Ahmed's skill will be playing at a level higher than England's second tier sooner than later.
Tajon Buchanan
Villarreal (Brampton, Ont.)
If Canada is to play up to its potential at the World Cup, Tajon Buchanan will also be playing up to his. As Buchanan goes, so does Canada. The 27-year-old winger uses his tenacity and pace to menace defenders on the right side. When Buchanan gets going, it's a long day at the office for those marking him.
Arriving in Europe in 2024 at Inter, Buchanan never settled in Italy, deployed at wing-back instead of his preferred role during his short stay in Serie A. His move to Villarreal, first on loan and then on a permanent, reinvigorated his game. The Brampton, Ont. native is now a regular starter on a Champions League side.
The World Cup marks Buchanan's return to a major tournament after his Copa America was cut short due to a shin injury. As Canada went on a run to the semis, Buchanan's tourney was over in the group stage. Buchanan's blistering speed is an asset that Marsch needs at his disposal.
Mathieu Choinière
Los Angeles FC (Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu, Que.)
A two-time MLS All-Star with CF Montreal, Mathieu Choinière is back in MLS with Los Angeles FC after a season with sister club Grasshoppers of the Swiss Super League. In a squad where players stand out with pace and tenacity, it's Choinière's measured approach in midfield that has made him a fixture in Marsch's teams. Capable of starting or coming off of the bench to manage a situation, Choinière can always be counted on for professionalism.
That's not to say Choinière doesn't bring other qualities to the pitch. A gifted player on set pieces, it's not uncommon to see the 27-year-old CFMTL academy product to stand over dead balls on both sides of the pitch. Through 15 matches this season, Choinière is well on his way to beating his 2023 career-best for goal contributions with seven.
Alphonso Davies
Bayern Munich (Edmonton)
What is there to say about Alphonso Davies? Canada's captain and talisman, the 25-year-old Bayern Munich star is, arguably, the most talented men's player Canada has ever produced. A serial winner, Davies has now captured seven Bundesliga titles, three DFB-Pokals and the 2020 Champions League crown in his eight seasons in Germany.
Like too many others on this Canada squad, Davies' 2025-2026 season has been defined by injury. Recovering from a torn ACL incurred in the spring of 2025, Davies made his season debut in December and would go on to miss further time with hamstring and ACL injuries. Currently recovering from his latest ailment, Davies is unlikely to be ready for Canada's June 12 opener.
Deployed mostly as a left-back at Bayern, Marsch has liked to use Davies in a more advanced role, capable of overlapping with the left-back or dropping back himself when the situation calls for it. Headed to a second World Cup, Davies went down in the record book as the first Canadian to score at a World Cup with his header against Croatia in the 2022 group stage.
Stephen Eustáquio
Los Angeles FC, on loan from Porto (Leamington, Ont.)
The straw that stirs the drink in Canada's midfield, Stephen Eustáquio wasn't getting the playing time he needed at Porto so he headed to Los Angeles FC on loan. The 29-year-old midfielder made 13 appearances across all competitions for LAFC, scoring once and providing an assist. Eustáquio's MLS stay was interrupted by a pair of freak injuries. He incurred a leg injury after colliding with a referee and then developed a hematoma and blood clot in his injured leg during a massage as part of his rehab process.
Now ready to go, Eustáquio is one of 15 players returning from Qatar 2022. The undisputed leader in Canada's midfield, Marsch sees an on-field general in Eustáquio. Few read a game the way Eustáquio is able.
Ismaël Koné
Sassuolo (Montreal)
If there was a breakout star for Canada at the 2022 FIFA World Cup, it was Ismaël Koné. A fine performance at Qatar springboarded Koné from Major League Soccer to Europe with Watford. After leaving Watford for Marseille, Koné is now in Serie A with Sassuolo was a major contributor in the promoted side's midtable finish. With his play opening the eyes of big clubs in Italy and abroad, the 23-year-old midfielder is ready for another strong showing on the world stage.
"I think we learned a lot from 2022," Koné told TSN. "We learned a lot individually, and as a team. We played such big opponents right away and we kind of learned a lesson. But now we're more well-rounded, we're a more complete group to go and execute what needs to be done."
Liam Millar
Hull City (Toronto)
Liam Millar will be playing Premier League football in August, but before he does that, he will be taking to the pitch in his hometown at the World Cup. The 26-year-old midfielder helped Hull City win the Championship playoff and head back to the top flight for the first time since 2017. A Liverpool youth product, Millar will become just the 13th player to play in the Premier League.
The Tigers' rise mirrored Millar's. Tearing his ACL in the fall of 2024, Millar fought back through a difficult rehab to make his return 11 months later. He made 33 league appearances for Hull, scoring three goals and setting up five more.
Capped 39 times by Canada, Millar is set for a second straight World Cup. Impressed by his motor, Marsch can slot Millar all over the midfield or even as a secondary striker. Millar knows there will be a fight for game time, but he's up for it.
Jonathan Osorio
Toronto FC (Toronto)
Likely one of the final players to stake his place in this Canada side, Jonathan Osorio enters the World Cup as an elder statesman at 34. Osorio needs just 11 more appearances to become only the second player to reach 100 caps for Canada. The Toronto FC captain and all-time leading appearance maker made his senior debut in 2013 and survived some lean times with the national program to reach a second straight World Cup.
No longer an automatic selection like he was in his earlier career, Osorio's value to the team comes from more than what he offers on the pitch. The MLS Cup winner is in his 14th pro season and provides the kind of leadership perfectly suited for a young team. Even if Osorio doesn't play, he can still make his mark this summer.
Nathan Saliba
Anderlecht (Longueuil, Que.)
When TSN asked Ismaël Koné who he foresees as the breakout star of Canada's World Cup entry, he didn't hesitate to give an answer: "Nathan Saliba, for sure." The 22-year-old CF Montreal academy product just completed his first season in Europe with Belgian giants Anderlecht. Starting 28 of 34 games, Saliba scored three goals and added an assist.
Having already made 13 appearances for Canada since his senior debut in 2024, the World Cup couldn't come at a better time for Saliba personally. Having already caught the eye of teams across Europe, a strong showing at the tournament could springboard Saliba to a big move this summer. The box-to-box midfielder could soon find himself plying his trade in one of the world's biggest leagues.
Jacob Shaffelburg
Los Angeles FC (Kentville, NS)
"The Maritime Messi" is set for his World Cup debut. Overcoming a leg injury in recent days, Jacob Shaffelburg has made the final Canada squad. It will come as a relief for a player who came perilously close to missing out after off-season groin surgery.
Making his Canada debut in 2020, Shaffelburg announced his presence on the international stage during the 2024 Copa America. The 26-year-old winger assisted on Jonathan David's marker in the 1-0 win over Peru, Canada's first victory at the tournament. He would go on to appear in all six of Canada's matches during Copa, scoring in victory over Venezuela in the quarters.
Like many of the other wing options on Canada, Shaffelburg has speed for days. But what sets him apart in Marsch's eyes is his unflinching nature. If Shaffelburg sees the pitch at the World Cup, you can be sure he will make the most of his minutes.
Jonathan David
Juventus (Brooklyn, NY)
Canada's all-time leading scorer, Jonathan David will look to brush off an uneven first season at Juventus to become the scoring threat that Jesse Marsch's side will need this summer. With 39 goals in 75 appearances, David is frequently in imperious form in a Canada shirt. A return to it will be a boon to his confidence after a club campaign in which he notched just eight goals in 46 appearances across all competitions.
With a resurgent Cyle Larin in the equation, Marsch is likely to employ both men up top and that will suit David just fine. Where David struggled at the Bianconeri was in his hold-up play. With Canada, he's not going to be asked to play a role in which he's not comfortable. Keeping David in and around the six-yard box will be a recipe for success.
Promise David
Union SG (Brampton, Ont.)
There are few players on this Canada squad who make football look more fun than Promise David. The fact that he's in the team at all is a testament to his drive and desire. Rupturing a tendon in his hip in February and requiring surgery, David looked to be a longshot to be fit in time for the World Cup, but he's managed to complete his rehab in time.
A driving force in Union SG's Belgian Pro League triumph last spring, the 24-year-old Brampton, Ont. native has scored 28 goals in 58 appearances across two seasons. Tall and strong, David is a handful for defenders and deceptively fast. His footwork is also impressive for a player of his build.
Where David is in his recovery remains to be seen. In the most likely scenario, David will be tasked with coming off of the bench with Canada chasing a goal or looking to hold on to a late lead. Having a weapon like David at his disposal is a welcome development for Marsch and one he, perhaps, wasn't expecting to have only a few weeks ago.
Marcelo Flores (UPDATE: Ruled out of World Cup with ruptured ACL)
Tigres (Georgetown, Ont.)
For the period in between the 2024 Copa America and the World Cup, Marsch said he would focus on players with dual nationalities to gauge their interest in playing for Canada. Marcelo Flores was one of them and Marsch obviously succeeded in making his case. The 22-year-old Arsenal academy product officially switched his allegiance to Canada after having previously made appearances for Mexico. It was a coup for Marsch after previous Canada manager, John Herdman, had actively recruited Flores to no avail.
Once again, it's Flores's flexibility that drew in Marsch. Capable of playing on the wing or more centrally, Flores is technically gifted, but also has a little bit of grit to his game. Probably most comfortable on the left where Canada is loaded with options, his ability to be deployed elsewhere can only help his chances of seeing significant minutes.
UPDATE: Flores will miss the World Cup after rupturing his ACL during Tigres' loss to Toluca in the CONCACAF Champions Cup Final on May 30.
Cyle Larin
Southampton, on loan from Mallorca (Brampton, Ont.)
As the calendar turned to 2026, there was a very real chance that Cyle Larin would miss out on a World Cup in his own country. The 31-year-old forward's loan at Feyenoord was a disaster and those he was competing with for a spot on the roster were receiving significantly more playing time. But a loan to Southampton changed all that.
The UConn product was a revelation in the Championship. Larin hit the ground running with Saints and scored eight goals in 16 league appearances, adding an assist. Larin helped Southampton march up the table into the playoff places. Saints would ultimately earn passage into the final at Wembley only for the EFL to boot them from the match after the club admitted to breaches of league regulations. While the season had a bitter end for Larin, it completely transformed his fortunes as he now heads into the World Cup as a presumptive starter.
Jayden Nelson
Austin FC
Yet another product of the footballing factory that is Brampton, Ont., Jayden Nelson has started over with Austin FC after an off-season trade from the Vancouver Whitecaps. The Toronto FC academy grad has already matched his career-high in MLS goals in only 10 matches with his new team. A part of the Whitecaps side that went to the MLS Cup Final, Nelson found minutes hard to come by down the stretch but is now a regular starter in Texas.
A pacey winger with technical skill, Nelson has been capped 12 times by Canada since his senior debut in 2020. He has yet to feature in 2026, largely due to the glut of talent at his position. Unlikely to feature much at this summer's tournament, the 23-year-old Nelson will still benefit greatly from the experience of being on the squad. He was a late replacement for the injured Marcelo Flores.
Tani Oluwaseyi
Villarreal (Mississauga, Ont.)
It's been a whirlwind last few months for Tani Oluwaseyi. After starting the 2025 season with Minnesota United and scoring 10 goals in 24 appearances, the 26-year-old forward made the jump to La Liga with Villarreal. In Spain, Oluwaseyi scored twice and added a pair of assists in his maiden campaign that saw the Yellow Submarine finish third in the table. Though Villarreal's Champions League campaign was one to forget, Oluwaseyi scored twice in Europe's top club competition.
Making his debut for Canada in 2024, Oluwaseyi has now scored twice in 22 appearances. Speedy and big, Oluwaseyi is the kind of player Marsch will relish throwing on late in a match against tired defenders to run them ragged. Built-in chemistry with club teammate Tajon Buchanan also won't hurt his chances of playing time.
_note: 全文走 summary 块A(LLM三判后只增不改);processed=成熟仓库累积,raw=本次收集过程。
